text: Summary of "World Development Volume 87, August 2017, 179–193" The globalisation agenda, the global pecking order of bilateral aid and even the current development policy are all based on the assumption of economic growth. And the momentum of this approach is reversing rapidly, in the light of growing social and ecological criticisms. Growing world GDP is one of the dominant factors in increasing global energy and resource consumption which can lead to destabilisation of the ecological frameworks supporting the Planet. As a response, post-growth theories and approaches are developing over the last several years. In this paper we present a comparative analysis of three of the most important Post-Growth theoretical perspectives; searching for possible links with their historical roots and evolution, as well as an assessment of the potential influence at the global level. Furthermore, we assess the possible tensions and convergences among the positions represented and we discuss the implications for the international debates on development policies by analysing the possible pathways and actors in the on-going process of challenging the conventional growth-based approach at the world level.
